properties.name: U.S. Route 66 — Joad family route (generalized)
properties.author: John Steinbeck
properties.note: Generalized alignment of U.S. 66 from Sallisaw, OK to the southern San Joaquin Valley, CA. Approximate; intended for classroom use, not navigation.

properties.name: Sallisaw, Oklahoma
properties.summary: Home, and the loss of it. Tom returns from prison to find the family tractored off the land. They buy a used Hudson and study a handbill promising fruit-picking work in California.
properties.q1_whats_where: Locate Sallisaw. Which part of Oklahoma is it in, and how close is it to the true Dust Bowl core in the Panhandle?
properties.q2_why_there: Why were tenant farmers — rather than landowners — the first people forced off this land when drought and banks squeezed the cotton economy?
properties.q3_relationship: What would make your family leave home? List three pushes and three pulls, then compare them with the Joads'.

properties.name: Oklahoma City
properties.summary: The family truck merges into the migrant stream. Chapter 12 leaves the Joads to describe Highway 66 itself — the 'mother road' — as a river of families flowing west.
properties.q1_whats_where: Trace Route 66 from here to Bakersfield. Which towns does it string together like beads?
properties.q2_why_there: Why does a single numbered highway matter so much in 1938? What did U.S. 66 offer a migrant family that back roads could not?
properties.q3_relationship: What is the 'mother road' of your own life — the route your family uses to reach work, family, or safety?

properties.name: Bethany, Oklahoma
properties.summary: Camped beside the Wilsons, Grampa suffers a stroke and dies. He is buried beside the road with a note in a jar, because a funeral would cost gasoline money.
properties.q1_whats_where: How far did the family travel on day one? Measure Sallisaw to Bethany along the route.
properties.q2_why_there: Why does leaving the land kill Grampa, in the logic of the novel? What is Steinbeck saying about the bond between people and place?
properties.q3_relationship: Is there a place whose loss would wound someone in your family the way leaving Sallisaw wounds Grampa? Where is it?

properties.name: Amarillo & the Texas Panhandle
properties.summary: Straight, shadeless road across the high flat Panhandle. Heat and distance wear on the truck, which is home, lifeboat, and largest asset at once.
properties.q1_whats_where: Describe the terrain around Amarillo using the map. What natural features are missing here that were present in eastern Oklahoma?
properties.q2_why_there: Why is this land flat, dry, and nearly treeless? What do elevation and rain-shadow geography have to do with it?
properties.q3_relationship: The truck is the family's most valuable possession. What single object does your household most depend on — and what happens where you live when it fails?

properties.name: Santa Rosa, New Mexico
properties.summary: The Wilsons' car throws a connecting rod. Tom and Al hunt the part in a wrecking yard. The family faces the road's hard arithmetic — and decides to hold together.
properties.q1_whats_where: Find the Pecos River. Where does it rise, and where does it go after Santa Rosa?
properties.q2_why_there: Why do wrecking yards, tourist camps, and repair shops cluster along Route 66 by 1938? Who is the migration an economy for?
properties.q3_relationship: When money runs short on a journey, who profits and who pays? Where do you see that same roadside economy today?

properties.name: Albuquerque, New Mexico
properties.summary: The route drops into the Rio Grande valley, then climbs into the western uplands — mesas and mountain distances, a reminder that 'the West' is many landscapes, not one.
properties.q1_whats_where: Locate the Rio Grande. Which two countries and how many U.S. states does it touch?
properties.q2_why_there: Why did Albuquerque grow exactly here — what do a river crossing, a rail line, and later a highway have in common?
properties.q3_relationship: The Joads pass communities far older than Oklahoma's farms. Whose migrations shaped the place where you live, and in what order?

properties.name: Flagstaff & the High Country
properties.summary: Past the Painted Desert into ponderosa pine at seven thousand feet — cool air and timber, an astonishment to a family from the plains. Ahead lies the drop to the Colorado.
properties.q1_whats_where: Compare elevations: Sallisaw, Amarillo, Flagstaff, Needles. Sketch the route's elevation profile.
properties.q2_why_there: Why are there pine forests here in the middle of the arid Southwest? What does altitude do to temperature and rainfall?
properties.q3_relationship: Where is the nearest place to you where the landscape changes that dramatically within a day's drive? What causes the change?

properties.name: Needles & the Colorado River
properties.summary: Green water at the edge of a white-hot desert. Noah walks away down the river; a deputy sneers 'Okie' at Ma; Granma begins to fail. California is across the water, but it is not a welcome.
properties.q1_whats_where: The Colorado forms the border here. Trace it upstream and downstream: what canyon lies above, and what sea (barely) receives it below?
properties.q2_why_there: Why is a river in the desert both a border and a magnet? Why do inspection and policing concentrate at crossings like this one?
properties.q3_relationship: The word 'Okie' turns a place of origin into an insult. What place-based labels attach to people in your region?

properties.name: Daggett & the Mojave Crossing
properties.summary: The family crosses the Mojave at night. At the agricultural inspection station Ma refuses to unload, pleading a sick grandmother — concealing that Granma has already died beside her.
properties.q1_whats_where: Measure Needles to Barstow. How many towns interrupt it? What does that emptiness mean for a 1938 truck?
properties.q2_why_there: Why does California inspect produce and plants at its border — what is the state protecting, and why is agriculture worth protecting that way?
properties.q3_relationship: Ma trades her own grief for the family's arrival. Who in the migrations you know of absorbed the cost so others could get through?

properties.name: Tehachapi Pass
properties.summary: At dawn the San Joaquin Valley opens below — orchards and vineyards, the promised land of the handbills. The family receives the view at the moment of its deepest loss.
properties.q1_whats_where: Find the pass. Which two mountain systems pinch together here, and what do they separate?
properties.q2_why_there: Why does the green begin so abruptly below the pass? Where does the valley's water actually come from, if not from rain?
properties.q3_relationship: Recall a place you first saw from above — did the view match what you found when you got down into it?

properties.name: Bakersfield & the Hooverville
properties.summary: Granma gets a pauper's burial. The family lands in a roadside Hooverville where handbills have summoned five workers for every job. Connie leaves; Casy is arrested in Tom's place.
properties.q1_whats_where: Hoovervilles appeared at the edges of towns. Why at the edges? What determines where the poorest people are allowed to stop?
properties.q2_why_there: Why did growers circulate more handbills than there were jobs? What does an oversupply of pickers do to the price of picking?
properties.q3_relationship: Where at the edge of your own town do newcomers with the least money live today? What put that neighborhood there?

properties.name: Weedpatch Camp (Arvin Federal Camp)
properties.summary: Clean water, toilets, Saturday dances, and committees of migrants governing themselves. Dignity, but not employment — hunger drives the family back onto the road. The camp still stands.
properties.q1_whats_where: Locate Weedpatch relative to Bakersfield and to the crops around it. What is grown in these fields today?
properties.q2_why_there: Why did the federal government build camps here, in the fields, instead of leaving housing to the growers or the towns?
properties.q3_relationship: This camp still shelters farmworker families almost ninety years later. What does it mean that the place outlived the novel — and the problem outlived them both?

properties.name: The Tulare Basin — Peaches, Boxcars, Flood
properties.summary: Strike-breaking work at a peach ranch; Casy is killed and Tom becomes a fugitive. The family picks cotton and winters in a boxcar camp until the rains come and the creek rises.
properties.q1_whats_where: Why does this basin flood? Find the rivers that drain into it and notice what the basin lacks: an outlet to the sea.
properties.q2_why_there: Why are pickers housed in boxcars beside the cotton? Who decided that, and what does housing tied to a job cost the worker?
properties.q3_relationship: The novel ends with one migrant family feeding a stranger. What networks feed and shelter people where you live when everything else fails?
